required.title: [For Student — Visual] A posted daily schedule

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] The Boxcar Children
suggested.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner

supplementary.title: [For Student — Visual] A kitchen timer or sand timer

competence_markers.description: They follow the daily rhythm — wake time, meal times, chore times, bedtime — consistently, without adult enforcement on most days
They complete their morning routine from wake to ready-for-the-day without step-by-step prompting on most mornings
They begin their daily chores at the correct time without being reminded — the time or event is the trigger, not the adult's voice
They clean up after activities automatically, without a separate instruction to do so
They demonstrate basic task sequencing — finishing one thing before starting another, doing required tasks before optional ones
They show respect for shared time — being ready when the family needs to leave, coming to meals when called the first time
They can use visual schedules, timers, or checklists as tools to support their rhythm, and are beginning to internalize the rhythm without needing the tools constantly
They engage in the weekly review honestly, identifying which parts of their rhythm are working and which need improvement

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through your morning routine.
narration_prompts.detail: What do you do first? What comes next? What part do you do without being reminded? What part do you still sometimes forget?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is your chore, and when does it happen?
narration_prompts.detail: Do you start it on your own, or does someone remind you? What would happen if you just forgot?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it feel like when your day has a good rhythm?
narration_prompts.detail: What does it feel like when things are out of order or you do not know what comes next?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you used a timer or a checklist.
What does "being ready on time" mean?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you think of a time you were ready? A time you were not? What was different?

narration_prompts.prompt: What part of your daily rhythm is the easiest to do on your own?
narration_prompts.detail: What part is the hardest? Why do you think that is?

cross_connections.description: The character foundation. IC1.1 built the internal willingness to follow routines and complete tasks. IC2.1 builds the external system — the schedules, the timing, the sequencing — that makes that willingness operational. A child with IC1.1 character but no IC2.1 system has the will but not the method. A child with IC2.1 systems but no IC1.1 character has the method but will not follow it when it is inconvenient.

The standard of "done" applies to every routine and chore in IC2.1. Clean-up is not just happening — it is happening to the right standard. The bed is not just made — it is made properly. IC2.2 provides the quality layer on top of IC2.1's timing layer.

The rhythm of the day creates the conditions for focused work. When the child knows what comes next and when transitions happen, they can give full attention to the current task instead of wondering what they should be doing.
The daily rhythm is tested hardest on hard days — when the child is tired, sick, disappointed, or frustrated. Maintaining the rhythm through difficulty is resilience in its most practical daily form.
Time is the first capital a person manages. The child who learns to manage time at age 6 is building the same executive function that manages money, resources, and effort at age 16.

As the child encounters screens and devices, the time architecture built here provides the structure that prevents digital distraction from consuming the day. IC2.1 establishes the rhythm; 4.2 addresses the threats to it.

content.why_this_matters:
Your day has a shape. You may not have thought about it before, but every day you live has a beginning, a middle, and an end — and the things you do inside that shape determine what kind of day it is. A good day is not a day where everything goes your way. A good day is a day that runs the way it is supposed to run — where things happen in the right order, at the right time, and nothing important gets skipped.

Right now, the adults in your life have built that shape for you. They decide when you wake up, when you eat, when you do your work, and when you go to bed. That is not because they are controlling you. It is because you are learning something that most people never learn well: **how to live inside a rhythm.**

A rhythm is when the same things happen at the same times, in the same order, every day. It sounds boring. It is not. It is the thing that makes everything else possible. The child who knows what comes next does not waste time wondering. The child who has a rhythm does not need someone standing over them telling them every next step. The child who lives inside a good rhythm becomes, over time, a person who can build their own rhythm — and that person can do things that the person without rhythm cannot.

This packet is about learning to live inside the rhythm — not because someone forces you, but because you understand that the rhythm is yours. It belongs to you. It is how your day works. And as you get older, you will be the one who designs it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Living inside a fixed daily rhythm** — wake, work, meals, chores, rest, and sleep happening at consistent, predictable times
2. **Morning and evening routines as owned sequences** — not just following steps, but knowing the steps and carrying them out independently
3. **Chore timing** — doing your responsibilities at the same time each day, without reminders, because that is when they happen
4. **Clean-up as automatic completion** — every activity ends with putting things back; the activity is not over until the space is restored
5. **Simple task sequencing** — understanding "first this, then that" as the basic architecture of getting things done
6. **Respect for shared time** — other people's schedules are real; being on time and ready is a form of respect
7. **Using tools for structure** — visual schedules, timers, and checklists as aids that help you stay on track
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Your Day Has a Shape

Every day has a shape. Not a shape you can draw, but a shape you can feel — the way one thing follows another, the way morning turns into afternoon and afternoon turns into evening.

Here is what a well-shaped day feels like:
- You know what comes first when you wake up
- You know what comes after breakfast
- You know when your work happens
- You know when your chores happen
- You know when you play or rest
- You know when the day winds down
- You know what bedtime looks like

When a day has this shape, you do not have to wonder what to do next. You do not have to be told. You already know — because the shape is the same every day. It becomes as automatic as breathing.

**When a day does NOT have a shape**, everything feels harder. You wander from one thing to another. You forget things. Someone has to keep telling you what comes next. You lose time without knowing where it went. The day feels scattered and unsatisfying, even if you did fun things.

The shape is not a cage. It is a track. A train on a track goes fast and far. A train off the track goes nowhere.

### Part 2: Fixed Times — The Anchors of Your Day

Some things in your day happen at the same time every single day. These are the anchors — they hold the shape of your day in place.

**Wake time.** You wake up at the same time every morning. Not when you feel like it. Not when someone drags you out of bed. At the time that is your time. Your body learns this time. After a while, you may even wake up before anyone calls you — because your body knows.

**Meal times.** Breakfast, lunch, and dinner happen at roughly the same time every day. You come to the table ready. You do not need to be called five times.

**Chore time.** Your chores happen at the same time each day. If you feed the animals in the morning, that is when you feed the animals — every morning. If you clean up after dinner, that is when you clean up — every dinner. The chore does not move because you do not feel like it.

**Bedtime.** You go to bed at the same time every night. The bedtime routine starts at the same time. The lights go out at the same time. This is not punishment. This is how your body rests properly and how tomorrow starts well.

These anchors are not flexible. They do not change because of your mood. They are the structure your day is built on. Everything else fits around them.

### Part 3: Morning and Evening Routines

Your morning routine and your evening routine are the two most important sequences in your day. They are the bookends — the opening and closing of each day. When they go well, everything between them tends to go well. When they fall apart, the whole day tends to fall apart.

**Your morning routine** is the sequence of things you do from the moment you wake up until you are ready for the day's work. It might look something like:

1. Wake up at your set time
2. Use the bathroom
3. Brush your teeth, wash your face
4. Get dressed
5. Make your bed
6. Come to breakfast
7. Do your morning chore

The order matters. The same order, every day, means you do not have to think about what comes next — your body knows. It becomes automatic. That is the goal: automatic. Not because you are a robot, but because you have freed your brain from having to make small decisions about things that should not require decisions.

**Your evening routine** is the sequence that closes the day:

1. Clean up from the day's activities
2. Prepare anything needed for tomorrow
3. Hygiene — brush teeth, wash up
4. Get into bed at the set time
5. Read or be read to (if that is part of your family's practice)
6. Lights out

**The test:** Can you move through your morning routine without anyone telling you what comes next? Can you move through your evening routine without reminders? If yes, the routine is becoming yours. If you still need prompting for every step, the routine is still the adult's — you are just following it.

### Part 4: Chores at the Same Time

You have responsibilities — chores that belong to you. Not chores you "help with" when asked. Chores that are yours.

IC1.1 established that you have chores and that you complete them fully. IC2.1 adds the time dimension: **your chores happen at a fixed time, and you begin them without being told.**

This is the difference between doing chores and owning chores. A child who does chores waits to be reminded. A child who owns chores knows when they happen and starts them because it is time — not because someone said so.

**Examples:**
- Feed the dog every morning right after breakfast — you do not wait to be told. Breakfast ends, you get up, you feed the dog.
- Set the table before dinner — you know when dinner is coming. You start setting the table before anyone asks.
- Take out the trash on Tuesday mornings — Tuesday morning, the trash goes out. You remember. You do it.

**Why the same time matters:** When a chore has a fixed time, it becomes part of the rhythm. It is not a random interruption — it is a predictable beat in the day. Predictable beats become automatic. Automatic beats require no willpower. And that is the goal: chores that cost you nothing mentally because they are built into the day's architecture.

### Part 5: Clean-Up as Completion

Every activity has an end. That end is not when you stop doing the activity. That end is when the space is restored to the condition it was in before you started.

- If you built with blocks, the blocks go back in the bin and the bin goes on the shelf
- If you drew pictures, the crayons go back in the box and the paper scraps go in the trash
- If you played outside, what came outside goes back inside
- If you cooked or baked, the counter gets wiped and the dishes get to the sink

**The activity is not over until the space is clean.** This is not a rule someone invented to annoy you. It is the way systems work. In any workshop, any kitchen, any laboratory, any military unit — the work includes the cleanup. The person who leaves a mess behind is not done. They abandoned the task before completion.

IC1.1 taught you to complete tasks fully. IC2.1 makes cleanup automatic — something that happens as naturally as the activity itself, without a separate instruction or reminder. You finish building, you put the blocks away. It is one continuous action, not two separate ones.

### Part 6: First This, Then That — Task Sequencing

One of the most basic skills in managing time is knowing what comes first and what comes second. This is called **sequencing** — putting things in the right order.

You already do this with your routines: first you brush your teeth, then you make your bed. First you eat breakfast, then you start your morning chore. The order is set. You follow it.

Now extend that idea beyond routines:
- First finish your work, then play
- First clean up this activity, then start the next one
- First do the hard thing, then do the easy thing
- First complete what you started yesterday, then begin something new

**"First this, then that" is the simplest form of time management.** It teaches you that things have an order, that the order matters, and that skipping ahead — doing the fun thing before the required thing — creates problems later.

When you are older, you will learn to build complex plans with many steps in many orders. But all of that starts here, with this simple principle: first things first.

### Part 7: Respect for Shared Time

Your day does not exist in isolation. Your family has a schedule. Your mentor has a schedule. Other people in your life have plans that depend on you being where you are supposed to be, when you are supposed to be there.

**Being on time is a form of respect.** When the family is leaving at 9:00, being ready at 9:00 means you respect everyone else's time. Making everyone wait because you did not get ready is not just inconvenient — it communicates that your time matters more than theirs.

**Being ready means being ready.** Not "almost ready." Not "I just need five more minutes." Ready. Shoes on, tasks done, standing at the door.

This is a small thing now. It becomes a large thing later. The adult who is chronically late — to meetings, to commitments, to deadlines — is communicating something about their character whether they intend to or not. The habit of respecting shared time starts here.

### Part 8: Tools That Help — Schedules, Timers, and Checklists

You do not have to hold everything in your head. That is what tools are for.

**Visual schedules** are pictures or words posted where you can see them — on the wall, on the refrigerator, by your bed. They show you what your day looks like. Younger students might have pictures. Older Stretching Branches students might have words. The schedule is a reference — you glance at it when you need to, not because someone points at it.

The goal is not to need the schedule forever. The goal is for the schedule to train your brain until your brain holds the schedule on its own. But there is no shame in using the schedule. Pilots use checklists. Surgeons use checklists. Using a reference tool is not weakness — it is professionalism.

**Timers** help you feel time passing. A sand timer sitting on the table while you do your work shows you: this is how long ten minutes looks. This is how long twenty minutes feels. Over time, your sense of time improves — you begin to feel when ten minutes has passed without looking at the timer. But the timer trains that sense. Without it, ten minutes and forty minutes feel the same to a young mind.

**Checklists** are lists of things to do that you mark off as you complete them. Your morning routine can be a checklist. Your chores can be a checklist. The satisfaction of checking something off is real — it is your brain saying "that is done, and I did it."

**The important thing:** These tools serve you. You do not serve them. The visual schedule, the timer, and the checklist exist to help you run your day well. As you get older and the rhythm becomes internal, you will need them less. But even adults who run complex lives use versions of these tools — calendars, planners, project trackers. The habit of using external tools to organize time is not training wheels to be discarded. It is a skill that scales.
content.formative_practices:
These are not one-time activities. They are ongoing daily and weekly practices that form the habits IC2.1 develops.

### Practice 1: Follow the Daily Rhythm [Daily]
Move through your day's fixed points — wake time, meal times, chore times, bedtime — at the correct times, in the correct order, without being reminded. Track this with your mentor: how many days this week did you hit every anchor point without a prompt? The measure is not perfection. It is trend — are you doing this more reliably this month than last month?

### Practice 2: Morning Routine Ownership [Daily]
Complete your entire morning routine — from wake to ready-for-the-day — without adult prompting for each step. You may use your visual schedule. You may not need a verbal prompt for each step. Each week, count: how many mornings did you get through the full routine on your own?

### Practice 3: Chore at the Right Time [Daily]
Begin your daily chore at its assigned time without being told. Not five minutes late. Not after a reminder. At the time, you start. This is the practice of internal triggering — the clock (or the event, like "right after breakfast") is the cue, not the adult's voice.

### Practice 4: Clean-Up Without Being Told [After Every Activity]
When you finish an activity, clean up before starting the next one. Without being reminded. Without being asked. The activity ends, the cleanup happens, the next thing begins. Practice this until it is automatic — until cleaning up feels like part of the activity, not something extra.

### Practice 5: Timer Awareness [3-4 Times Per Week]
Use a timer during tasks. Before you start, guess how long the task will take. Set the timer. Do the task. Check: were you close? This is the earliest form of time estimation — a skill that IC2.1-B will develop much further. For now, just start building the sense of how long things actually take.

### Practice 6: Weekly Review [Weekly, With Mentor]
At the end of each week, sit with your mentor for 5-10 minutes. Review: Did you follow your daily rhythm most days? Where did you need reminders? Which routines are becoming automatic? What is still hard? This is not a report card. It is a conversation about how your system is working.

### Practice 7: Respect for Shared Time [As Opportunities Arise]
When your family has a departure time, a meal time, or any shared commitment — be ready on time. Not almost ready. Ready. Track this informally: are you the person who is ready, or the person everyone waits for?

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Build the Architecture, Then Transfer It

IC2.1-A follows a clear developmental arc:

1. **You build the schedule.** For the youngest students (4-5), the daily rhythm is entirely designed and maintained by the adult. The child lives inside it.
2. **You make it visible.** The schedule is posted, the timers are used, the transitions are marked. The child can see the architecture.
3. **You enforce it consistently.** The same times, the same order, every day. The architecture is real only if it holds.
4. **You begin stepping back.** As the child internalizes the rhythm, you reduce prompting. Not all at once — one piece at a time. The morning routine first, then chore timing, then cleanup.
5. **The child runs the system.** By late Stretching Branches, the child moves through their day with minimal prompting. The architecture is theirs.

This transfer does not happen in weeks. It happens over months and years. Rushing it produces a child who can perform the routine when supervised but collapses when the adult steps away. Patient transfer produces a child who genuinely owns their daily system.

### Design the Day

The daily rhythm should include these fixed anchors:

- **Wake time** — same time every day, including weekends (within reason; a small weekend variation is acceptable, a two-hour shift is not)
- **Morning routine** — a defined sequence from wake to ready-for-the-day
- **Meal times** — consistent, predictable
- **Chore times** — each chore has a time or event trigger (e.g., "right after breakfast" or "before dinner")
- **Work time** — when schoolwork, learning, or practice happens
- **Free time** — when play or self-directed activity is appropriate
- **Evening routine** — a defined sequence from end-of-day through lights-out
- **Bedtime** — non-negotiable

The specific times will vary by family. The consistency of the times will not. Whatever schedule you establish, hold it. The rhythm the child internalizes is the rhythm you actually maintain — not the one you intend.

### Use Physical Tools

At this age, external tools are not a crutch — they are the mechanism of internalization.

**Visual schedules:** Post a daily schedule at the child's eye level. For ages 4-5, use pictures or picture-word combinations. For ages 6-9, use simple written schedules. The child should be able to glance at the schedule and know what comes next without asking. Update the schedule if the routine changes — an outdated schedule is worse than no schedule, because it teaches the child that posted information is unreliable.

**Timers:** Use physical timers — sand timers, kitchen timers, or simple digital timers the child can see. Not phone timers hidden in a pocket. The point is that the child sees time passing. "You have fifteen minutes before we leave" becomes tangible when the child can watch the sand run or the numbers count down. Over time, this builds an internal sense of duration that most children (and many adults) never develop.

**Checklists:** A checklist for the morning routine. A checklist for chores. The child marks each item as completed. The act of checking off a box creates a micro-completion that reinforces the habit of sequential task completion. Keep checklists simple — 5-8 items maximum for young children.

### The IC1.1/IC2.1 Boundary

Parents and mentors often ask: "How is this different from IC1.1?" The answer is in the focus:

- **IC1.1** is about the child's will — can they comply? can they finish? can they stop? can they wait? The formation is internal.
- **IC2.1** is about the child's system — does the day have a rhythm? do they know when things happen? can they sequence tasks? do they have tools? The formation is operational.

A child who refuses to do their morning chore has an IC1.1 issue (character, compliance). A child who intends to do their morning chore but does it at 11:00 instead of 7:00 because they lost track of time has an IC2.1 issue (time awareness, routine integrity). Both need attention, but the interventions are different. IC1.1 issues require formation of the will. IC2.1 issues require formation of the system.

In practice, they overlap heavily at Stretching Branches because the young child is developing both simultaneously. But keeping the distinction clear helps you diagnose problems accurately.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Daily Rhythm Consistency

**Developing well:** The child moves through the day's fixed points — wake, meals, chores, bedtime — at the correct times without adult enforcement on most days. They may glance at the visual schedule. They do not need repeated verbal prompts about what time things happen.

**Developing slowly:** The child requires constant reminders about when things happen. "It's time to feed the dog" happens every morning at the same time, yet the child never initiates it. The daily rhythm exists only because the adult enforces every transition.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the rhythm actually consistent? (If you are inconsistent about meal times or chore times, the child cannot internalize what is not stable.) Is the visual schedule posted and current? Is the child getting enough sleep to function? (An overtired child cannot self-manage.) Is the child in a developmental stage where the level of self-initiation expected is realistic? (A 4-year-old needs more prompting than an 8-year-old.)

### 2. Morning Routine Independence

**Developing well:** The child completes their morning routine — wake to ready — without step-by-step prompting on most mornings. They may need a single prompt ("Have you started your routine?") but not a sequence of instructions for each step. The routine takes a consistent amount of time.

**Developing slowly:** Each morning is a fresh battle. The child stalls between steps, requires prompting for every transition, and the routine takes wildly varying amounts of time depending on adult involvement.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the routine too complex? (Simplify for younger children — 5 steps, not 10.) Is the visual checklist clear and posted where the child sees it immediately upon waking? Has the routine been stable long enough for internalization? (A routine changed last week cannot be internalized this week.) Is there a consistent consequence when the routine is not completed — not punishment, but the natural result: you don't move to the fun thing until the routine is done?

### 3. Chore Self-Initiation

**Developing well:** The child begins their chore at the designated time without being told. The time or triggering event (e.g., "after breakfast") produces the action without adult intervention.

**Developing slowly:** The child waits to be told, every time, even though the chore has been the same, at the same time, for weeks or months.

**Key insight:** Self-initiation is the specific skill IC2.1 adds beyond IC1.1. The IC1.1 child will do the chore when told. The IC2.1 child does the chore because it is time. If the child is compliant but never self-initiating, IC1.1 is working but IC2.1 is not yet. The intervention is to make the time trigger explicit ("Your chore happens right after breakfast — I should not need to tell you"), withdraw prompting gradually, and allow natural consequences (the chore still needs to be done, and the delay means less free time).

### 4. Automatic Cleanup

**Developing well:** Activity ends, cleanup begins — without a separate instruction. The child treats cleanup as part of the activity, not as a distinct task imposed afterward. The transition is smooth and largely self-directed.

**Developing slowly:** The child finishes the activity and moves to the next thing. Cleanup only happens when explicitly directed. There is no internalized connection between "activity ends" and "space is restored."

**How to build this:** The connection must be taught explicitly and enforced consistently. "We do not start the next thing until we finish the current thing, and finishing includes cleanup." Every single time. No exceptions. The consistency of enforcement is what creates the automatic link. If cleanup is sometimes required and sometimes skipped, the child learns that cleanup is optional — and optional things are, by definition, not automatic.

### 5. Task Sequencing

**Developing well:** The child demonstrates understanding of "first this, then that" in their daily life. Required tasks before optional ones. Current tasks completed before new ones begun. The concept of order is applied practically without needing it restated each time.

**Developing slowly:** The child cherry-picks activities in whatever order appeals to them. Fun things first, required things deferred or forgotten. There is no evident internal sense that order matters.

### 6. Respect for Shared Time

**Developing well:** The child is ready when the family needs to leave. They come to meals when called. They begin transitions when told it is time, rather than requiring repeated prompts.

**Developing slowly:** The child is consistently the person everyone waits for. Departure times are missed because the child was not ready. Being ready on time requires adult micro-management of every preparation step.

**How to build this:** Make the cost visible. "We are leaving at 9:00. That means you need to be dressed, teeth brushed, and at the door at 9:00. If you are not ready, we will discuss what went wrong — but the family does not wait." The natural consequence of poor time preparation (missing part of the outing, having to rush, or being the person everyone waited for) is more instructive than any lecture.

### 7. Tool Use

**Developing well:** The child uses their visual schedule, timer, or checklist as a reference. They glance at the schedule to confirm what comes next. They are aware of the timer running. They check off their list items. The tools are serving their purpose.

**Developing slowly:** The tools are ignored. The visual schedule hangs on the wall unread. The timer goes off and the child does not respond. The checklist is never marked.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the tools well-designed? (A cluttered, hard-to-read schedule will not be used.) Are they placed where the child naturally sees them? Has the child been taught how to use them, or were they just put up and expected to work? Is the child old enough to use the specific tool? (A complex written schedule is not useful for a 4-year-old who cannot read.)

### 8. Weekly Review Engagement

**Developing well:** The child participates in the weekly review and can identify which parts of their rhythm are working and which are not. They know where they needed reminders and where they did not.

**Developing slowly:** The child gives vague answers or cannot articulate anything about how their week went. "Fine" or "I don't know" are the primary responses.

**How to improve:** Be specific. Instead of "how did your week go?" ask "how many mornings did you start your routine without me reminding you?" or "which chore did you start on time every day this week?" Specific questions produce specific awareness. Share your own observations: "I noticed you fed the dog on your own every morning this week. That's different from last month."
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Maintaining the Schedule You Stated

The most common IC2.1 failure at Stretching Branches is the same as the most common IC1.1 failure: **adult inconsistency.** If you establish a 7:00 AM wake time but allow sleeping until 8:00 on weekdays when it is convenient, the child learns that 7:00 is a suggestion. If chore time is "after breakfast" but some mornings you skip it because the day is busy, the child learns that chore time is optional.

The schedule the child internalizes is the schedule you actually maintain. Not the one posted on the wall. Not the one you intend. The one you enforce.

### Prompting vs. Reminding vs. Doing It For Them

There is a difference between a prompt, a reminder, and doing it for the child:
- **A prompt** is a single, brief cue: "Morning routine." It activates the child's own knowledge of what comes next.
- **A reminder** is step-by-step narration: "Brush your teeth. Now wash your face. Now make your bed." This is the adult running the routine, with the child as a passive participant.
- **Doing it for them** is making the bed yourself because it is faster.

IC2.1-A aims to move from reminders to prompts to no prompts. If you are still providing step-by-step reminders after months, the transfer is not happening. Diagnose why: Is the routine too complex? Is the visual schedule unclear? Are you inconsistent about requiring independent execution?

### Over-Scheduling

A daily rhythm is not a minute-by-minute itinerary. Young children need unstructured time — time to play, explore, and be bored. The fixed anchors (wake, meals, chores, bedtime) provide the structure. The spaces between the anchors should include genuine free time. An over-scheduled child is not learning time management — they are learning to be managed.

### Confusing IC2.1 with IC1.1

If the child knows when their chore happens and still refuses to do it, that is an IC1.1 problem (character, will, compliance). If the child is willing to do their chore but never initiates it because they have no internal sense of when it should happen, that is an IC2.1 problem (time awareness, system design). The interventions are different. Conflating the two leads to frustration on both sides.

### Timer Dependency vs. Timer Training

The point of timers is to train the child's internal sense of time — not to create a permanent dependency on external alarms. If the child cannot function without a timer after months of use, the timer is being used as a crutch rather than a training tool. Begin withdrawing the timer gradually: "How long do you think that will take? Let's see if you're right — without the timer this time."

### Weekend Collapse

Weekends test whether the rhythm is internalized or merely enforced. If the child's entire daily structure collapses every Saturday and Sunday — sleeping until 10:00, skipping chores, abandoning routines — the weekday structure was external, not internal. The rhythm should hold on weekends, with appropriate flexibility (a slightly later wake time is reasonable; a completely different day is not). The weekend version of the rhythm should be designed and maintained, not abandoned.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Morning Routine
**Strong:** Sequential, detailed, shows ownership. "I wake up, go to the bathroom, brush my teeth, get dressed, make my bed, then eat breakfast." Can identify what they do well and what they forget sometimes. Speaks about it as their routine, not something done to them.
**Weak:** "I get ready." No sequence, no detail. Cannot identify what they do independently vs. what requires prompting.

### Prompt 2: Chore Timing
**Strong:** Knows the chore, knows when it happens, describes self-initiation. "I feed the chickens right after breakfast. I just get up and do it." Can describe what happens if they forget — not theoretical consequences, but actual experienced ones.
**Weak:** "My mom tells me to do stuff." No ownership of timing. Cannot connect the chore to a time or triggering event.

### Prompt 3: Rhythm Feeling
**Strong:** Can articulate the difference between a structured day and an unstructured one. "When my day goes right, I know what comes next and everything feels easy. When it doesn't, I forget things and get behind." This is remarkable self-awareness for a young child — do not expect it before age 7-8.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish between days with rhythm and days without. This is normal for younger Stretching Branches students and develops with time.

### Prompt 4: Tool Use
**Strong:** Can describe using a specific tool and how it helped. "I look at my checklist in the morning and check things off. It helps me not skip anything." Shows the tool serving a function.
**Weak:** "I don't know" or references tools that exist but are not used. The tool is decorative, not functional.

### Prompt 5: Shared Time
**Strong:** Can describe what "being ready" means concretely and has examples of both success and failure. Shows awareness that their readiness affects others.
**Weak:** "I don't know" or "I'm always ready" (contradicted by observation). No awareness of the impact of their timing on others.

### Prompt 6: Easiest and Hardest
**Strong:** Honest and specific. Can name what is automatic and what requires effort. "Making my bed is easy — I just do it. But starting my chore after breakfast is hard because I want to keep playing." This kind of self-knowledge is the foundation for Weathering Storms self-management.
**Weak:** "Everything is easy" or "everything is hard" — no differentiation, no self-awareness about specific patterns.
